gpt_review.biasedText: They are not, contrary to widespread belief, universally eager to turn over every last shred of their intellectual and community-building capacity to robot servants/overlords.
gpt_review.reason: The author does present his limited observations as a corrective to 'widespread belief,' which is a form of opinion stated with factual authority. The 'robot servants/overlords' phrasing also carries loaded connotations.

gpt_review.biasedText: This all runs counter to the doomy picture of helplessness painted last spring and summer by well-circulated stories about generative A.I.'s effect on higher education.
gpt_review.reason: This matches my detection of spin/loaded framing in characterizing prior coverage as a 'doomy picture of helplessness painted.'

While this is a direct quote from Stuart Selber, the author chose to include it without any counterpoint. The word 'panic' does characterize others' concerns dismissively, though it's attributed to a source.

gpt_review.biasedText: a horseshoe-crab-like relic of primordial ed tech that A.I. has saved from extinction
gpt_review.reason: The colorful language does carry a subjective tone about traditional tools, though in context it reads more as stylistic humor than outright bias. Still, a defensible detection of non-neutral word choice.

gpt_review.biasedText: All of the bigger forces in our culture are pulling people away from deeply attentive reading
gpt_review.reason: This is a quote from Scott Saul, but the author presents it uncritically. It is a sweeping causal claim presented without evidence.

gpt_review.biasedText: Such tech-assisted breezing through a text is to reading a novel roughly as listening to a podcast about marriage is to being married.
gpt_review.reason: The analogy is dramatic and emotionally loaded, designed to trivialize tech-assisted reading. It's the author's own characterization and could be seen as sensational framing.

gpt_review.biasedText: the ultraprocessed flavor of A.I. in a paper
gpt_review.reason: The metaphor 'ultraprocessed flavor' carries a clearly negative connotation, borrowing from food discourse about unhealthy products. It's loaded language that reveals the author's stance.

gpt_review.biasedText: students ... are also more anxious and isolated
gpt_review.reason: The author actually balances this by noting students are 'more professional and accomplished' in the same sentence. This is not negativity bias when both positive and negative traits are mentioned.

gpt_review.biasedText: break the funereal hush of isolatoes bent in solitude over their phones
gpt_review.reason: The language is evocative and clearly negative in depicting students on phones. 'Funereal hush' and 'isolatoes' are pejorative characterizations that reveal bias against phone use.

gpt_review.biasedText: The cellphone and now A.I. have made college a lonelier experience than it used to be
gpt_review.reason: This matches my own detection. It's a contested causal claim presented as established fact.

gpt_review.biasedText: Students feel pressure to use college to prepare intensively for a particular career, like training full-time as an amateur to play a sport professionally, but they will graduate into a working world where the games and rules keep changing.
gpt_review.reason: The claim about the job market's unpredictability is debatable and the analogy imposes the author's viewpoint as if it were self-evident truth.

gpt_review.biasedText: after first going fetal for far too long when assailed by return-on-investment literalists who willfully misunderstand the fit between school and work
gpt_review.reason: Matches my own detection. The loaded language 'going fetal,' 'literalists,' and 'willfully misunderstand' are dismissive characterizations of critics.

gpt_review.biasedText: This tech-obsessed moment may have finally pushed humanists to identify the value of their disciplines and play to their strengths, after first going fetal for far too long when assailed by return-on-investment literalists...
gpt_review.reason: This overlaps with my bias by omission detection. The author frames humanists as sympathetic victims under unfair attack and never substantively engages with the opposing reform perspective.
